OE2: Although I am not personally affected, I know others who have had issues with food insecurity and the biggest complaints that I have heard are that the food pantries are not open enough hours and that it is embarrassing to go to them. Perhaps having more available hours and some sort of pickup option would rectify this. For example, if people are embarrassed to go to the food pantry then why not set up a website for it where people can pick a time that they want to get food and have volunteers run it out to their car. Additionally, the pantry is not open enough days or hours. It just is not practical that people who are already struggling with food issues (and probably many other issues as well) all be expected to be available with reliable transportation for that 1 to 3 hour window on a Wednesday or Thursday. Ask for more volunteers and donations and increase the hours.

Experience:
I struggle to pay the bills because I have had a neck injury and severe chronic pain for over a year now and it has made my life very stressful.  I had to quit my high paying job and now I deliver food when I can to pay the bills.  I was short last month and I'm really scared I am going to have to have surgery and I won't be able to work at all.  I was denied for unemployment and denied disability.  
I'm blessed to have food stamps but inflation is rough.  I have a car but it needs a lot of work and I need it for delivering food because it's the only job that doesn't make my injury pain worse because I am able to sit comfortably and it doesn't have really long hours.
I'm back in school again it's like I have to start over, and I have even less time to make money.  Next month I have an appointment at the UNM Neurology surgery department and I'm scared but glad to be seeing someone who can finally help me.

Obstacles: I am fortunate to qualify for SNAP, but only because I am also working part-time with my original employer from before I was a student, while being a full-time student.  Many of my fellow students were denied food assistance because they, also being full-time students, did not have any employment.  The graduate program caps outside employment of students to 15 hours/week.  It seemed incredibly unfair that I, someone with access to some disposable income, qualifies for SNAP, but my fellow students who do NOT have access to disposable income, do NOT qualify for food stamps.  I was of the belief that the SNAP program exists to help people who are striving towards better opportunities, such as an upper-level education, but due to time or logistics limitations, cannot support themselves financially, even before considering tuition expenses.  To NOT make it available to graduate students in an in-demand field seems contrary to that entire premise.

Obstacles:
Financial Constraints: One of the main obstacles that affect people's ability to access healthy food is financial constraints. Healthy food options such as fresh fruits and vegetables, lean protein, and whole grains are often more expensive than unhealthy options. For people who are on a tight budget or living in poverty, it may be challenging to afford healthy food consistently.
Geographic Location: Access to healthy food can also be limited based on geographic location. In some areas, there may be limited or no grocery stores that offer fresh, healthy food options. This can be particularly challenging for people who don't have access to transportation to travel to other areas to purchase healthy food. This is commonly known as a "food desert."

Experience: I lost my housing over summer when my partner suddenly dumped me. At about the same time, I was accepted into my academic program. I was assured the loans would cover costs of living and that moneys had been adjusted for inflation, which turned out to not be true. So I found an acquaintance to rent from in exchange for picking up after her and her animals and deep cleaning the whole house twice a month in addition to rent. Going into finals week, she gave me notice solely because she prefers living alone. This hurt my academic performance on finals. I also lost about 7% of my body weight that fall semester due to a lack of finances. I had to choose whether to prioritize rent or food, and I chose rent because I was afraid of eviction, which happened regardless and without reasonable justification. I never brought friends over, was remarkably quiet, and minded what hours I came/left and used the kitchen. There was nothing more I could have done to keep my living situation. I ended up hungry and with housing insecurity during finals week, despite all my best efforts to avoid those circumstances.

Foodprog: A food bank or food pantry (including a campus food pantry)
Obstacles: I'm a graduate student--the pay isn't high enough for me to regularly eat healthy food, even after the 7.12% increase this semester, because of my rent and medical expenses. I work at .5FTE and take 9-12 credit hours each semester, plus regular service work for my department, and trying to fit my own research in there somewhere, which means I do not have the time to pursue a regular second job, even though I am still doing freelance work whenever I can to supplement my income. Also, the campus food pantry selection is often spotty.
Foodavail: Purchasable food is not affordable enough. The food pantry selection is often not great.
Placesslept: Campus or university housing,In a rented or owned house, mobile home, or apartment (alone or with roommates/friends),In a closed area/space with a roof not intended for human habitation (abandoned building; car/truck/van/RV/camper; encampment or tent; unfinished garage, attic, or basement; etc.)
Housingdiff_why: It's expensive: as a graduate student at .5FTE and taking a full load of classes (which prohibits regular supplemental income), I can't afford by myself to rent most apartments, all houses, and all university housing. The apartments I can afford go quickly, and quite a few agencies require you pay for an application (usually with no refund) of thirty, fifty, seventy-five dollars before you can even see the place you're applying for. What's in my price range is a room in someone else's house/apartment, where I'm essentially living with a co-renter and/or my landlord. I haven't been able to find anyone in my program who needs a roommate, and I'm not willing to date someone for housing again, so I'm left with the latter option. A lot of people who are renting a room to a student/young professional for rates I can afford aren't actually willing to create a lease, which further narrows the list of options; I am a trans person, so not only do I need to put in more effort than the average person in screening potential landlords, but I absolutely need whatever legal protection I can get from a lease, because I cannot risk being at the mercy of a landlord who could react in a discriminatory or even dangerous way if they find out I'm trans. This isn't even narrowing it down to people who I'd find it peaceful to live with or places that I'd enjoy living in--the financial and safety factors make my list of options vanishingly small and difficult to locate right off the bat.
Strategies: Borrowing money from family or friends,Engaging in sexual activity for food or housing,Going on a date in exchange for food or housing,Taking food that has been discarded or left behind,Dumpster-diving,Seeking out events with free food,Sleeping in a car,Something else not listed (please share)
Strategies_other: Eating plants on campus/in parks sometimes. Staying with a spouse who made my life miserable.
Experience: More than once I've found myself going to grade my students' work for my assistantship and I stall out cause the next meal I need to worry about is hanging over my head; they don't get the focus I deserve when I'm hungry. Same thing in my classwork, when it's near the end of the month or if I've had a big expense and I've got to stretch stuff sometimes, it's just so frustrating to be here studying the things I'm devoting my life to and know that I can't put out anything more than mediocre work. It's hard to pick just one day or experience--the times that I'm skipping meals or (way more frequently) just eating unhealthy and unsatisfying food because it's what I can afford, they just stretch out and settle around me like a fog, and I'm vaguely aware that if I could eat what I wanted to then I'd be doing better, I'd be happier and more focused, but I can't right now, so I don't; so I have to live with being forgetful, being tired. I make stupid mistakes--I'll misread what students wrote, I'll forget the due dates that are right in front of me, I'll have trouble just keeping things organized, and I know I'm making things worse for them and I just have to swallow how much I hate myself for it and how much they deserve better. In the classes I'm taking, I get more puzzled or concerned looks or comments from the professors, because I'm visibly tired in class, whereas I'm really eager to participate when I'm eating better. So not only am I struggling to complete my assignments, I'm also attracting embarrassing attention, and worrying about whether or not this is affecting the possibility of receiving mentorship from this or that professor or whether they've even subconsciously put me in a box marked "unstable--do not trust with opportunities". Or, worse yet, a box marked "easily bored and not serious as a scholar." And then my paycheck comes in, and I eat better for a couple weeks with more vegetables, more things that I don't have to force down my throat because I'm so tired of them (e.g., things that aren't Ramen), and the fog clears up and I go back to being able to do what I need to and enjoy it. If I don't have a big expense like a car repair or a rent deposit or a medical treatment or having to help out a family member financially or the price of gas going up insanely high or something else, and/or if I do get a freelancing gig or two that I have enough time to take, then I can eat OK until the next paycheck. But that doesn't always happen.

Disability_other: I am transgender and taking hormone replacement therapy.
Expenses: I work at a job that isn't a work-study job (including self-employment),I work as a Teaching Assistant or Research Assistant,I get help from family or friends,I use savings
OE2:
Create on-campus housing specifically for graduate students (although this would be even more effective if created for all students) at no more than $550/month including utilities. 

Raise graduate assistantship minimums (yes, again--yes, it really is that bad). Raise undergraduate pay rates for work-study positions. 

Incentivize departments to provide summer funding for graduate students, especially for international students who cannot work off-campus, and incentivize departments to directly and successfully assist US-citizen graduate students with summer job placement outside the department. 

Set aside more money specifically for the UNM food pantry and for providing UNM students with free, ready, healthful meals on a weekly basis (perhaps involving a voluntary contribution by President Stokes from the UNM Foundation funds that are set aside in "reasonable," discretionary amount for her use towards private gifts [section 5.b.vi of her employment agreement], or from the section in her employment agreement that details that the University will pay for all entertainment expenses at University House that benefit the University [section 6.b]). 

Work aggressively and effectively to decrease the cost of other significant expenses that students incur at UNM (parking and tuition come to mind), especially for the students who are in the most precarious situations.

However, in the current economy, there is even more pressure. Financial stress does not just go away; it is something that you are constantly stressed about. It is something you are always aware of, so it makes focusing in class, on assignments, or in meetings difficult. OE2:
I would love a service where students and employees could pre-order lunch or other meals for the week and have those meals delivered somewhere on campus. This way, dining services would know how much food they need and have guaranteed income. I work through lunch on most days, so having the ability to pre-order lunches and know they would be delivered to my office would be amazing! 

I'm unsure how the university can help housing insecurity beyond having dorms and apartments available on campus, which they already do. It would be fantastic if the city had a program with rental/relator companies and town apartments that would offer students lower rental rates. I understand this would be a huge undertaking and very hard to accomplish, but it would really help so many students!

Housingdiff_why: There is no university available graduate student housing. It is possible to be mixed with undergraduates, but there are clearly differences between these populations. Off-campus, the surrounding 10-12 blocks in all directions are owned by private or company landlords that continue to increase rent without any material improvements to property or meeting basic safety concerns. During the pandemic my landlord sent me a letter that they were increasing the rent by $150/month. When I asked for an explanation, I received a statement that the cost of everything was going up and so was rent. This was a slap in the face, using people's shelter for profit is despicable, but that is what happens all around our campus. Landlords come in and purchase up properties and then privately rent them so there is not even the guarantees of equal housing rules. When applying for the place I live now the landlord asked questions about my sexual identity and religion, which seeing the cross hanging around his neck I knew he was really asking if I was straight and christian. I'm a queer atheist, but I said what was needed to be able to be offered the place. The University should advocate and push policy at the state level that strips the protections of these landlords because profit over people is no way to lead.

Obstacles: As a grad student, I dedicate the majority of my time to my studies and my job through the school. Unfortunately, even with the income I receive I struggle to make ends meet. Approximately half of my salary has to go to living expenses, and the rest of my funds are directed to paying for grad school at NMSU. I struggle to stay afloat on a month to month basis. I make too much to qualify for food assistance programs, but a large proportion of my salary is funneled directly back to the school for tuition. This is an unfair aspect of being a grad student at NMSU.

Because of my financial insecurity, I cannot afford to relocate.

Institution: New Mexico State University, Las Cruces
Foodprog: A food bank or food pantry (including a campus food pantry)
Obstacles: Between my academic, TAship, and other professional obligations, I am often at school from 7am and 7pm on week days. This makes it difficult to find time to cook proper meals, so I sometimes have to rely on pre-packaged food which is more expensive and less nutritious. When that runs out, I cannot afford to purchase food on campus so I'm just stuck.
Foodpantry_reasons: The hours of operation for the campus food pantry do not work for me,I am not sure I am eligible to use the campus food pantry
Foodavail: Food on campus is very expensive without a meal plan, which I don't think I'm eligible for. For a meal consisting of a drink, a sandwich, and a side at Einstein bagels in Corbett or Panda Express in the bookstore, the cost is around $10-$12. Meals are somewhat cheaper at the Frenger Food Court, but those hours are narrower and often incompatible with my schedule. If I were to eat lunch every day on campus, I could not afford to eat any other meals.
Placesslept: Campus or university housing
Housingdiff_why: It's very difficult to find affordable housing in Las Cruces - costs are high near campus, most places do not offer utilities, and spaces are very small to share with roommates. I've accepted the higher cost of living on campus because I did not have the time during business hours to call every single complex in town, nor could I afford $25-$50 application fees to merely be considered. I do not meet income requirements (as an NMSU teaching assistant) to rent in places which are comparable to on-campus housing in size and cost.
Strategies: Borrowing money from family or friends,Seeking out events with free food,Something else not listed (please share)
Strategies_other: eaten meals with friends who have more
Experience: I have a chronic illness which sometimes makes food preparation difficult. Recently as I was experiencing a flare-up, I purchased prepared food (frozen meals, canned soup) to try and prevent this from affecting my academics. Unfortunately, I wasn't able to bring these meals to campus because I do not have a freezer in my department and the microwave was out of operation. As a result, I could not eat during school hours, which for me last week were approximately 9am to 9pm. When I got home I was sometimes too tired even to stick soup in the microwave. By Thursday I could not focus, and I had to put off doing homework that was due soon. After I finally got some rest on Saturday, I pulled an all-nighter from Sunday to Monday to try and catch up. I do not currently have food in my house and can't afford to fix that, and I am wondering what I'll do to get dinner tonight, if anything. High costs and lack of access to food preparation on campus, together with my illness, snowballed a bad couple of days into a painful couple of weeks with no clear path forward.
Trans_pub_notuse: Public transportation does not go where I need to go,I don't know how to access public transportation
Disability: Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc),Living with a mental health condition
Expenses: I work as a Teaching Assistant or Research Assistant,I get help from family or friends,I use savings
OE2: NMSU needs to pay student workers, including both undergraduate and graduate student workers, a better wage. NMSU should staff the Aggie Cupboard outside of regular business/school hours at least once a month. NMSU should look for ways to decrease costs of the "efficiency" housing available to upperclassmen, and subsidize family housing for international students and others who have only one working adult in the home. On-campus shopping and dining options should include low-cost, healthy foods like fresh fruits and prepackaged foods. There should be microwaves available in common spaces like department lounges and student offices. Programs like the emergency assistance fund through the Dean of Students should be widely advertised and not buried in an old page that hasn't even been connected to the website since the Cascade changeover. Faculty and staff who have contact with students should be made aware of resources for students both on and off campus. Vitamins should be available at the AHWC pharmacy. "Searching for transitional/emergency housing" and "experiencing domestic violence" should qualify as excusable absences for students. And I'll say it again just to be clear: the university needs to pay student workers more.
OE3: It is impossible for me to work a second job while working a TAship and taking full-time classes. But if I don't take full-time classes, I can't have a TAship, and if I don't have a TAship, I can't take the resident tuition discount, so I have no choice except to depend fully on the university. NMSU needs to recognize the responsibility it has to students in my position and take steps to support us directly and consistently. Lotteries for scholarships, like the one that was made available to graduate students who completed orientation or the one presented to graduate students who attended a volleyball game (in person, without an audience mask mandate) last semester, are an insult to students facing insecurity. I am disappointed at how little effort NMSU has made to lobby the NM state legislature during this session to advocate for the economic support listed above. I recently helped an undergraduate student access emergency shelter and was heartbroken both by the situation and by the fact that most of the resources we identified were outside the university because the school did not have a program in place to support them. This student may lose their scholarship because of academic issues directly related to their housing insecurity. Why is it incumbent on me - an underpaid graduate assistant - and not the financial resources of a multi-million dollar university system to get this student housed and protect their academic standing in the face of challenges beyond their control? I am so sick of "financial workshops" and info boards telling me how to budget and to try buying less coffee when the school has the ability to solve my budgeting problems.
